title: NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence Pack Readiness
exclusions: Does not determine legal applicability, complete regulator submissions, approve policy decisions, or replace counsel or competent-authority review; does not attest to the legal sufficiency of pack contents.
evidence: Saved search `UC-22.2.35`, dashboard drilldown to `_audit` events, optional `index=audit_evidence` export, and time-aligned `grc:audit_finding` records for supervisory traceability.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.32 is assigned contributing assurance because this UC provides concrete Splunk evidence for the observable control condition, while policy approval, legal interpretation, and official filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.32 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.35: NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence Pack Readiness.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.35' running on sourcetype scheduler and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Art.33 is assigned contributing assurance because this UC provides concrete Splunk evidence for the observable control condition, while policy approval, legal interpretation, and official filings remain outside Splunk.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.33 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.35: NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence Pack Readiness.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.35' running on sourcetype scheduler and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: For a catalogued job deliberately disabled for one business day, `_audit` lacks a successful `action=search` row within the 7-day window and the alert returns that `savedsearch_name`, empty `last_runtime`, and a null or failed `result` plus the configured owner from the lookup.
controlTest.negativeScenario: For a catalogued job that completed successfully each day, the joining `_audit` stats show a fresh `last_runtime` within SLA and `result=success`, so the row is excluded after the post-filter even when neighbouring jobs in the same app fail.

dataSources: `index=_audit` and `index=_internal` with `sourcetype=scheduler` for skip/fail telemetry; `index=grc` `sourcetype=grc:audit_finding` (fields `finding_id`, `status`, `owner`, `due_date`); `index=itsm` `sourcetype=snow:sn_grc_policy` for policy/evidence tasks; optional export landing checks in `index=audit_evidence`. Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) supplies normalized ServiceNow fields when ITSM/GRC tables are ingested.
app: Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud Platform; Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow ([Splunkbase 1928](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/1928)) for `snow:sn_grc_policy`-style feeds.
spl:
| inputlookup nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv
| join savedsearch_name type=left [
    search index=_audit action=search earliest=-7d
    | stats latest(total_run_time) as last_runtime latest(info) as result by savedsearch_name
]
| where isnull(last_runtime) OR match(result,"(?i)fail|error|skipped")
| lookup nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions savedsearch_name OUTPUT exception_until
| where isnull(exception_until) OR now()>strptime(exception_until,"%Y-%m-%d")
| table savedsearch_name, owner, last_runtime, result
description: Flags scheduled Splunk saved searches that contribute to supervisory evidence packs when they did not run in the last week or finished with failure, skip, or error — the operational heartbeat for automated compliance artefacts before a regulator or internal audit requests them.
value: Directors and operational risk teams get an early warning when the automation that produces inspection-ready material has broken, so legal and GRC can intervene before a deadline rather than explaining missing attachments after the fact.
implementation: (1) Maintain `nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv` with one row per evidence job (`savedsearch_name`, `owner`, product scope). (2) Ingest GRC/task correlation via Splunkbase 1928 if ServiceNow owns evidence workflows. (3) Schedule this search daily with a 7-day window. (4) Route alerts to the named owner and archive pass/fail snapshots to `index=audit_evidence`. (5) Optional: mirror PDF/CSV outputs with a scripted output that writes to WORM — see `outputs.conf` on the search head.
visualization: Table of failing or stale jobs keyed on `savedsearch_name`, single value count of open gaps, timeline of `last_runtime` by owner.

references.title: Splunk Docs — Monitor scheduler health (internal indexes)

knownFalsePositives:
• **ServiceNow GRC rename drift** — Distinguish: `_audit` shows success but `grc:audit_finding` still references the old job label. Suppress: update `nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv` under change control; use `lookup nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions` with a 14-day time-bound exception for cutover.
• **Planned indexer or search-head maintenance** — Distinguish: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=scheduler` shows skips tied to known hosts in a maintenance roster. Suppress: join a `maintenance_windows` lookup on `splunk_server` and add `where in_window=0` to the alert SPL.
• **Legal hold pausing reports** — Distinguish: GRC ticket documents intentional pause. Suppress: maintain an exception register column `legal_hold` on the catalogue; filter with `where legal_hold!="true"` until counsel closes the hold.
• **Intentional throttle during a major incident** — Distinguish: incident commander record in ServiceNow points at disaster search head queue. Suppress: time-bound exception row in `nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions.csv` reviewed every 48 hours.
equipmentModels: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase 1928, GRC compliance evidence)
Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud (REST API for saved search health, _internal for evidence generation)
Splunk Enterprise Security 7.x (Splunkbase 263, correlation search status)
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `_audit` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions`, `nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions','nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.32`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.32 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.35: NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence Pack Readiness." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `_audit` under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain `nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv` with one row per evidence job (`savedsearch_name`, `owner`, product scope). The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** Ingest GRC/task correlation via Splunkbase 1928 if ServiceNow owns evidence workflows. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule this search daily with a 7-day window. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route alerts to the named owner and archive pass/fail snapshots to `index=audit_evidence`. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Optional: mirror PDF/CSV outputs with a scripted output that writes to WORM — see `outputs.conf` on the search head. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence Pack Readiness," the aggregation is the evidence artifact itself — auditors will read these rows as the official record of NIS2 control activity.

This search begins with `| inputlookup nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup nis2_evidence_report_catalog.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true nis2_evidence_report_catalog_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the last 7 days) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.2.35: NIS2 Supervisory Compliance Evidence Pack Readiness]
cron_schedule = 0 2 * * 1
dispatch.earliest_time = -7d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.2.35,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `last_runtime`, `owner`, `result`, `savedsearch_name`. Run:

```spl
index=_audit sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table last_runtime, owner, result, savedsearch_name
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup nis2_evidence_kfp_exceptions
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.2.35: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Failing Or Stale Jobs Keyed On `Savedsearch Name` (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Single Value Count Of Open Gaps (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Timeline Of `Last Runtime` By Owner (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We check whether the scheduled jobs that build your regulator-ready paperwork actually ran on time. If a job failed or never finished, we raise a hand so the right owner can fix it before anyone official asks for the files.
